narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of past students, focusing on factors like academic records and test scores to forecast bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 33.50 (lower than the usual 36.70), and your placement in the lowest decile for grades in both Year 1 and Year 3 of school—and predicted a 60.5% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially. This elevated risk of failure is why your full-time law program application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass the bar on their first attempt to maintain strong outcomes. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking at the bottom, which strongly signaled potential struggles in sustaining strong performance compared to peers who ranked much higher on average. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which, while not the lowest possible, pulled in the opposite direction by somewhat supporting a passing prediction relative to even weaker scores. Your Year 1 decile at the bottom also contributed notably toward the failure risk, reinforcing concerns about early academic challenges that typically hinder bar success. Overall, your grades placed you well below the middle of the pack where most admitted students sit, outweighing positives like your full-time enrollment plans, which are common among 93% of applicants. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment highlighting where your metrics fell short of those who historically pass. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the 36.70 average, as even a modest improvement could shift predictions favorably. Strengthening your academic profile through additional coursework or a post-bac program might also help elevate your decile rankings in future evaluations. Many students in similar situations reapply successfully after targeted improvements, so reviewing your transcripts for patterns and seeking advice from a pre-law advisor could make a real difference. With persistence, you can reshape how models like this one view your bar exam prospects.
